exercises.sourceTaskName: Exercises, Task 1: Complete the Conditional Sentences (Types I, II, III)

promptParts.after: out with your friends tonight, I will watch the football match on TV.

questions.grammarRule: First Conditional if-clause uses simple present: 'if you go out...', not 'will go'.

promptParts.before: If you go out with your friends tonight, I

questions.grammarRule: First Conditional main clause uses 'will' + infinitive, matching the if-clause 'go' (simple present): 'I will watch...'.

promptParts.after: a lot of money if I get that job.

questions.grammarRule: First Conditional main clause uses 'will' + infinitive: 'I will earn...'.

promptParts.before: I will earn a lot of money if I

questions.grammarRule: First Conditional if-clause uses simple present: 'if I get that job'.
First Conditional if-clause negative uses 'doesn't' + base verb: 'if she doesn't hurry'.
First Conditional main clause uses 'will' + infinitive: 'we will miss the bus'.

promptParts.after: harder, he would reach his goals.

questions.grammarRule: Second Conditional if-clause uses simple past: 'if he tried harder'.
Second Conditional main clause uses 'would' + infinitive, matching the if-clause 'tried': 'he would reach his goals'.
Second Conditional main clause uses 'would' + infinitive: 'I would buy these shoes'.

promptParts.before: I would buy these shoes if they

questions.grammarRule: Second Conditional if-clause uses simple past. 'Fit' is unusual — its past tense is spelled the same as the present ('fit'), so it doesn't change form here.

promptParts.after: me if he did not know the answer.

questions.grammarRule: Second Conditional main clause negative uses 'would not' + infinitive: 'it would not surprise me'.

promptParts.before: It would not surprise me if he

questions.grammarRule: Second Conditional if-clause negative uses simple past: 'if he did not know'.

promptParts.after: to the radio, we would have heard the news.

questions.grammarRule: Since the main clause is 'would have heard' (Third Conditional), the if-clause needs past perfect: 'had listened'.

promptParts.before: If we had listened to the radio, we

questions.grammarRule: Third Conditional main clause uses 'would have' + past participle, matching the if-clause 'had listened': 'we would have heard the news'.

promptParts.after: on the lights, you would not have fallen over the chair.

questions.grammarRule: Since the main clause is 'would not have fallen' (Third Conditional), the if-clause needs past perfect: 'had switched'.

promptParts.before: If you had switched on the lights, you

questions.grammarRule: Third Conditional main clause negative uses 'would not have' + past participle: 'you would not have fallen'.

promptParts.after: to our party if she had not been on holiday.

questions.grammarRule: Third Conditional main clause uses 'would have' + past participle: 'she would have come to our party'.

promptParts.before: She would have come to our party if she

questions.grammarRule: Since the main clause is 'would have come' (Third Conditional), the if-clause needs past perfect negative: 'had not been'.

exercises.sourceTaskName: Task 2: Write the verbs in brackets in their correct forms into the gaps

promptParts.after: a minute, I'll come with you.

questions.grammarRule: First Conditional if-clause uses simple present: 'if you wait a minute'.
Second Conditional main clause uses 'would' + infinitive, matching the if-clause 'went': 'it would be Australia'.

promptParts.after: TV if Peter hadn't bought the theatre tickets.

questions.grammarRule: Since the if-clause is 'hadn't bought' (past perfect negative, Third Conditional), the main clause needs 'would have' + past participle: 'would have watched'.

promptParts.before: Would you go out more often if you
promptParts.after: to do so much in the house?

questions.grammarRule: Second Conditional if-clause negative uses simple past: 'if you did not have to...'.

promptParts.before: If he hadn't missed the train, he

questions.grammarRule: Since the if-clause is 'hadn't missed' (past perfect negative, Third Conditional), the main clause needs 'would have' + past participle: 'would have arrived'.

promptParts.before: I will miss the school bus if I

questions.grammarRule: First Conditional if-clause negative uses 'do not' + base verb: 'if I do not get up early'.

promptParts.before: We could have helped John if we

questions.grammarRule: Since the main clause is 'could have helped' (Third Conditional pattern), the if-clause needs past perfect: 'had known'.

promptParts.after: to Dresden, it will be a fantastic trip.

questions.grammarRule: First Conditional if-clause uses simple present: 'if we cycle to Dresden'.

promptParts.after: enough money, they would buy a new car.

questions.grammarRule: Second Conditional if-clause uses simple past: 'if they had enough money'.
First Conditional main clause negative uses 'will not' + infinitive: 'I will not play football'.

promptParts.after: you if I hadn't seen you today.

questions.grammarRule: Since the if-clause is 'hadn't seen' (past perfect negative, Third Conditional), the main clause needs 'would have' + past participle: 'would have phoned'.

promptParts.before: We would pass the exam if we

questions.grammarRule: Second Conditional if-clause uses simple past: 'if we learnt more' (British spelling of 'learned').

promptParts.after: to the party if you come with me.

questions.grammarRule: First Conditional main clause uses 'will' + infinitive, matching the if-clause 'come' (simple present): 'I will go to the party'.

promptParts.after: the cat, their children would have been happy.

questions.grammarRule: Since the main clause is 'would have been happy' (Third Conditional), the if-clause needs past perfect: 'had bought'.

promptParts.before: You'll catch a cold if you

questions.grammarRule: First Conditional if-clause negative uses 'don't' + base verb: 'if you don't wear a pullover'.

promptParts.after: the film, I wouldn't have bought the video.

questions.grammarRule: Since the main clause is 'wouldn't have bought' (Third Conditional), the if-clause needs past perfect: 'had seen'.
